# Summary {.unnumbered .unlisted}

**Title:** *Heterogeneity in hormonal responses from the oral glucose tolerance test among individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes: The ADDITION-PRO study*

**Introduction:** Individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes are more heterogeneous than standard glycemic classification captures. While distinct patterns of body fat distribution have been identified among metabolic subgroups, how the interplay between postprandial incretin and glucagon responses associates with body fat distribution remains unclear. This study aimed to identify metabolic subgroups based on postprandial hormonal responses and to quantify their associations with body fat distribution.

**Research design and methods:** K-means clustering was applied to 1,381 individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes from the ADDITION-PRO cohort. Measures of glucose, incretins (glucagon-like peptide-1 and glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide), insulin sensitivity and glucagon suppression were used as clustering variables. Visceral (VAT) and subcutaneous (SAT) adipose tissue and body fat percentage were assessed by ultrasonography and bio-electrical impedance analysis. Associations between clusters and standardized measures of fat distribution were assessed through linear regression models adjusting for age, sex, smoking and physical activity.

**Results:** Three clusters were identified. Cluster 3 was characterized by the highest glucose levels, lowest insulin sensitivity, highest VAT, SAT, VAT/SAT ratio and body fat percentage (0.68, 0.28, 0.18 and 0.63 SD units higher than cluster 2, respectively). Clusters 1 and 2 had similar glycemic profiles but differed in incretin and glucagon suppression; cluster 1 had the lowest incretin secretion but highest glucagon suppression, while cluster 2 showed the opposite pattern. Cluster 1 had similar VAT to cluster 2 but higher SAT (0.25 SD units) and body fat percentage (0.15 SD units), and a lower VAT/SAT ratio (-0.23 SD units).

**Conclusion:** K-means clustering identified three subgroups with distinct incretin and glucagon responses. The subgroups also differed in body fat distribution, particularly in VAT and SAT. As findings are limited by the cross-sectional design, future studies should examine whether this heterogeneity predicts different risk trajectories for type 2 diabetes and different responses to prevention and treatment strategies.
